name: 36 · Audio Transcribe (VAD → data channel → streamed parts → gather)
description:
Exercises BOTH docs/25 planes end-to-end: a binary DATA channel carries the audio, and a CONTROL channel streams the transcribed parts which a consumer-edge `join: gather` folds into one transcript.

  Start{audio: file} → vad (retrieve WAV, energy-gate, re-emit voiced PCM on Data/Out `speech`) → transcribe (stream('speech') → faster-whisper tiny → emit each segment on Control/Out `parts`) → collect (gather `parts` → full transcript) → End{transcript}.

The audio is a real uploaded file, NOT base64. The Start `audio` field is `kind: file`, so `start.audio` is a `FileRef`; the `vad` step calls `aithericon.file(start.audio['key']).retrieve()` to broker the bytes from the object store through the sidecar (the child holds no storage creds), reads the WAV with stdlib `wave`, runs a pure-Python `audioop` RMS energy gate over 30 ms frames, and writes ONLY the voiced frames as raw 16-bit PCM (~100 ms per write) over the `speech` data channel. The bulk audio rides the out-of-band JetStream datastream transport — it NEVER enters the net marking; the net sees only the channel's open + close (2 firings for the whole stream). The `transcribe` step drains the byte stream via `stream('speech')` (starting EARLY, while the VAD step is still producing), reassembles the PCM, and runs faster-whisper (`tiny`, CPU/int8). Whisper yields a generator of segments, so instead of returning one string it EMITS each segment as it lands onto the Control/Out channel `parts` via `aithericon.out('parts')` — one bracketed episode (open → item* → close), one tiny `{index,text,start,end}` token per segment. The producer does NOT decide the fold; the `transcribe → collect` edge carries `join: gather`, so the compiler's gather barrier re-orders the segments by index and parks the collection as `{output: [...]}`. The `collect` step reads that off `input.output` and concatenates the segment texts into the final `transcript`. Control-plane streaming + consumer-side fold, the data-plane analog of demo 17.

Audio is a friendly payload for the JetStream data channel: a 30 ms 16 kHz frame is ~960 bytes at ~50/s — far under the message cap, so no S3 adapter is needed (S3 is for multi-GB elements like 4K frames). The data plane is durable + reliable-ordered, which is exactly right for transcription (no dropped speech).

LIVE-ONLY, run via the API (not `mekhan test`): the `transcribe` step first pip-installs faster-whisper + ctranslate2/numpy and downloads the `tiny` model (~75 MB) into the venv/HF cache, which exceeds the 60 s test cap on a cold run; warm runs are fast. A self-contained sample is bundled at `assets/sample.wav` (generated via macOS `say`, says 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. Streaming audio over a data channel works.'). Run it with one command — `mekhan run demos/36-audio-transcribe --start-tokens demos/36-audio-transcribe/sample-input.json` — where the bundled `sample-input.json` ships `{"audio": {"__file": "assets/sample.wav"}}`; the CLI uploads the file and substitutes the FileRef (the same `__file` convenience the demo asset seeder uses). A longer clip with ~1.8 s silence gaps between sentences is also bundled at `assets/sample-long.wav` (run it with `--start-tokens demos/36-audio-transcribe/sample-long-input.json`) — it exercises the energy gate visibly: ~20.5 s in → ~12 s of voiced audio kept (the silence is dropped from the `speech` stream, never transmitted). The compile shape (binary data channel) is covered offline by demo 14; this demo's value is the live audio round-trip + transcription.
